question: You respond to a 68-year-old male who was eating breakfast when his wife noticed his face looked droopy on one side. He tried to speak but his words were slurred and he could not lift his right arm. His wife called 911 immediately and states symptoms began exactly 45 minutes ago. He has a history of hypertension atrial fibrillation and type 2 diabetes. His medications include metoprolol warfarin metformin and lisinopril. Vital signs are BP 178/96 HR 88 irregular RR 16 SpO2 97% on room air. On exam he has right facial droop with forehead sparing right arm weakness with drift and dysarthria. What is the significance of the forehead sparing in his facial droop?
option_a: Forehead sparing indicates the facial weakness is from a peripheral nerve injury like Bell palsy
option_b: Forehead sparing indicates an upper motor neuron lesion consistent with stroke because the forehead receives bilateral cortical innervation
option_c: Forehead sparing means this is not a stroke
option_d: The forehead is always spared in facial weakness
rationale: The forehead muscles receive innervation from both cerebral hemispheres so a stroke affecting one hemisphere will spare forehead movement because the other hemisphere can still innervate those muscles. In contrast peripheral facial nerve palsy like Bell palsy affects the entire half of the face including forehead because all fibers to that side travel through the damaged nerve. This patient's presentation with forehead-sparing facial droop arm weakness and dysarthria is classic for acute stroke. The atrial fibrillation is a major risk factor for cardioembolic stroke.

question: Continuing with the previous patient you perform a stroke assessment using the Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale. What are the three components of this scale and what is the significance of an abnormal finding?
option_a: Arm drift speech and pupil response. One abnormality suggests possible stroke
option_b: Facial droop arm drift and speech. One abnormality has 72% probability of stroke and all three abnormal has greater than 85% probability
option_c: Blood pressure heart rate and oxygen saturation
option_d: Pupil response grip strength and verbal response
rationale: The Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale assesses three components: facial droop by asking patient to smile arm drift by having patient hold arms out with eyes closed and speech by having patient repeat a sentence. If any one component is abnormal there is a 72% probability of stroke. If all three are abnormal probability exceeds 85%. This simple rapid assessment helps identify stroke patients who may benefit from time-sensitive interventions like thrombolytics. This patient has all three components abnormal making stroke highly likely.

question: You are transporting the stroke patient to a comprehensive stroke center 25 minutes away. Medical control asks for the NIHSS score but you have not been trained in this assessment. They instead ask for the LAMS score. The patient has facial droop cannot hold his right arm up at all and cannot grip with his right hand. He has no leg weakness. What is the LAMS score and what does it indicate?
option_a: LAMS 0 indicating minor stroke
option_b: LAMS 4 indicating likely large vessel occlusion requiring thrombectomy evaluation
option_c: LAMS 10 indicating massive stroke
option_d: LAMS cannot be calculated without CT scan
rationale: The Los Angeles Motor Scale LAMS is a prehospital tool to identify large vessel occlusion LVO strokes that may benefit from mechanical thrombectomy. It scores facial droop 0-1 arm drift 0-2 and grip strength 0-2 for a total of 0-5. A score of 4 or greater suggests high probability of LVO. This patient scores 1 for facial droop 2 for arm falls immediately and 1 for weak grip for a total LAMS of 4 suggesting possible LVO. This information helps determine destination hospital as LVO strokes need comprehensive stroke centers capable of thrombectomy.

question: A 72-year-old female presents with sudden onset of difficulty speaking. Her husband states she was talking normally then suddenly began making sounds that do not make sense. She appears frustrated when trying to communicate. She can follow simple commands and has no facial droop or arm weakness. Vital signs are BP 168/94 HR 78 RR 16 SpO2 98% on room air. What type of aphasia is this patient demonstrating and what area of the brain is likely affected?
option_a: Receptive aphasia from damage to Wernicke area in the temporal lobe
option_b: Expressive aphasia from damage to Broca area in the frontal lobe. She understands but cannot produce meaningful speech
option_c: Global aphasia from damage to both language areas
option_d: Dysarthria from cerebellar damage
rationale: Expressive or Broca aphasia occurs when the patient understands language and knows what they want to say but cannot produce meaningful speech. Words may be garbled nonsensical or absent. Patients are often frustrated because they know they are not communicating properly. Broca area is located in the posterior inferior frontal lobe usually in the left hemisphere. In contrast receptive or Wernicke aphasia involves inability to understand language with fluent but meaningless speech production. This patient can follow commands indicating intact comprehension but cannot speak properly indicating expressive aphasia.

question: A 65-year-old male is found by his daughter who states she spoke to him on the phone 6 hours ago and he was fine. She found him on the floor unable to move his left side. He is awake but confused about what happened. She does not know exactly when symptoms began. Vital signs are BP 188/102 HR 82 RR 18 SpO2 96% on room air. He has left facial droop left arm and leg weakness with neglect of his left side. For time-sensitive stroke treatments what is the significance of the unknown time of onset?
option_a: Unknown time of onset does not affect treatment decisions
option_b: The last known well time is 6 hours ago which may exclude him from IV tPA but advanced imaging at the hospital may identify patients who can still benefit from thrombectomy
option_c: He cannot receive any stroke treatment because onset time is unknown
option_d: Assume onset was just before he was found and he is eligible for all treatments
rationale: For IV thrombolytics tPA the traditional window is 3 to 4.5 hours from symptom onset. When onset is unknown the last known well time is used which is 6 hours in this case placing him outside the tPA window. However mechanical thrombectomy can be performed up to 24 hours in select patients based on advanced imaging showing salvageable brain tissue. CT perfusion or MRI can identify mismatch between infarcted core and at-risk penumbra. This patient should still be transported emergently to a stroke center for imaging evaluation as he may benefit from thrombectomy despite the unknown onset time.

question: You are transporting a 58-year-old female with acute stroke symptoms that began 90 minutes ago. She has right-sided weakness and aphasia. Her blood pressure is 204/118. Medical control asks about blood pressure management. What are the blood pressure parameters for potential tPA candidates and what is the danger of aggressive blood pressure reduction in acute stroke?
option_a: Blood pressure should be lowered to normal 120/80 immediately
option_b: For tPA eligibility BP must be below 185/110. However aggressive BP reduction can worsen stroke by decreasing perfusion to ischemic penumbra
option_c: Blood pressure does not matter for stroke treatment
option_d: Higher blood pressure is always better in stroke
rationale: Blood pressure management in acute stroke is nuanced. For patients who may receive tPA blood pressure must be below 185/110 before administration and below 180/105 after. However aggressive blood pressure lowering is dangerous because elevated BP may be a compensatory mechanism to maintain perfusion to the ischemic penumbra. Dropping BP too quickly can extend the infarct. For patients not receiving tPA permissive hypertension up to 220/120 is generally allowed unless there is another indication for treatment like aortic dissection or hypertensive emergency with other end-organ damage.

question: A 70-year-old male had sudden onset of left-sided weakness and facial droop 2 hours ago. At the emergency department CT scan shows no hemorrhage. He is given IV tPA. During transport to the ICU he develops severe headache becomes less responsive and his right pupil becomes dilated and nonreactive. Blood pressure increases to 210/120 and heart rate drops to 52. What complication has likely occurred?
option_a: The tPA is working and the stroke is resolving
option_b: He has developed hemorrhagic transformation with intracerebral hemorrhage and possible herniation. The tPA should be stopped immediately and reversal agents considered
option_c: He is having a seizure
option_d: He is experiencing a migraine headache
rationale: Hemorrhagic transformation is the most feared complication of tPA occurring in approximately 6% of patients with symptomatic hemorrhage in about 2-3%. Signs include sudden neurological deterioration severe headache vomiting and signs of increased intracranial pressure. The dilated nonreactive pupil with Cushing triad of hypertension bradycardia and irregular respirations suggests uncal herniation from mass effect. Management includes stopping tPA immediately administering cryoprecipitate and tranexamic acid to reverse fibrinolysis emergent CT scan and neurosurgical consultation for possible hematoma evacuation.

question: A 55-year-old female presents with the worst headache of her life that began suddenly 3 hours ago while exercising. She describes it as a thunderclap headache that reached maximum intensity within seconds. She has vomited twice and has a stiff neck. She has no focal weakness. Vital signs are BP 168/98 HR 92 RR 18 SpO2 98% on room air. She is photophobic and has pain with neck flexion. What type of stroke is most likely and what is the underlying cause?
option_a: Ischemic stroke from carotid artery stenosis
option_b: Subarachnoid hemorrhage most commonly from ruptured cerebral aneurysm
option_c: Hemorrhagic stroke from hypertension
option_d: Transient ischemic attack
rationale: Subarachnoid hemorrhage SAH presents with sudden severe thunderclap headache often described as the worst headache of my life. The headache reaches maximum intensity within seconds unlike migraines which build over minutes to hours. Associated symptoms include nausea vomiting photophobia and meningismus neck stiffness due to blood irritating the meninges. The most common cause is rupture of a cerebral aneurysm. SAH can occur without focal deficits initially. Diagnosis is by CT scan which is 95% sensitive in first 24 hours followed by lumbar puncture if CT is negative. Rebleeding is a major concern with high mortality.

question: A 78-year-old male with a history of poorly controlled hypertension presents with sudden onset of severe headache left-sided weakness and decreasing level of consciousness over the past hour. His daughter states he was fine this morning then complained of headache and progressively became weaker and more confused. Vital signs are BP 228/134 HR 68 RR 14 SpO2 95% on room air. Left pupil is 6mm and sluggishly reactive. Right pupil is 3mm and reactive. He has dense left hemiplegia. What type of stroke is most likely and why is blood pressure management different than for ischemic stroke?
option_a: Ischemic stroke. Allow permissive hypertension to 220/120
option_b: Intracerebral hemorrhage from hypertension. Blood pressure should be reduced more aggressively to SBP less than 140 to reduce hematoma expansion
option_c: Subarachnoid hemorrhage. Blood pressure does not require treatment
option_d: Transient ischemic attack that is resolving
rationale: Intracerebral hemorrhage ICH presents with sudden focal neurological deficits progressive deterioration headache and signs of increased ICP. Hypertension is the most common cause with bleeding typically in the basal ganglia thalamus pons or cerebellum. The anisocoric pupils and declining consciousness suggest mass effect and possible early herniation. Unlike ischemic stroke where permissive hypertension preserves penumbral blood flow in ICH elevated BP promotes hematoma expansion. Current guidelines recommend SBP reduction to less than 140 mmHg for ICH patients presenting with SBP 150-220 using IV antihypertensives like nicardipine or labetalol.

question: A 62-year-old male presents with sudden onset of vertigo nausea vomiting difficulty walking and slurred speech. He has no headache or weakness. On exam he has horizontal nystagmus that is worse when looking to the left ataxic gait with falling to the left and dysarthria. Finger to nose testing shows dysmetria on the left. Vital signs are stable. He has a history of hypertension and diabetes. What area of the brain is likely affected and why is this presentation concerning?
option_a: This is benign positional vertigo and is not a stroke
option_b: This suggests a cerebellar stroke which is concerning because the posterior fossa is a confined space and cerebellar edema can cause brainstem compression and hydrocephalus
option_c: This is labyrinthitis and not a stroke
option_d: This is a migraine with aura
rationale: Cerebellar strokes present with vertigo ataxia nystagmus dysarthria and dysmetria without the hemiparesis seen in hemispheric strokes. The HINTS exam can help differentiate central from peripheral vertigo. Cerebellar strokes are particularly dangerous because the posterior fossa is a closed space. Cerebellar edema can compress the brainstem causing coma and death or obstruct CSF flow causing hydrocephalus. Patients can deteriorate rapidly and may require emergent suboccipital craniectomy. Any patient with acute vertigo and neurological findings should be evaluated for posterior circulation stroke.

question: A 70-year-old female presents with right arm weakness and difficulty speaking that began 2 hours ago. Her symptoms have now completely resolved and she feels back to normal. She wants to refuse transport stating it was probably nothing since she feels fine. Her history includes hypertension hyperlipidemia and smoking. Vital signs are normal. On exam she has no deficits. What was the likely event and why should she be transported for evaluation despite symptom resolution?
option_a: She had a panic attack and does not need evaluation
option_b: She likely had a transient ischemic attack TIA which is a warning sign for impending stroke. Her 90-day stroke risk is significant and she needs urgent evaluation and risk factor modification
option_c: Since symptoms resolved she does not need any treatment
option_d: This was a migraine aura and is not concerning
rationale: A transient ischemic attack TIA is a brief episode of neurological dysfunction caused by focal brain ischemia without infarction. Symptoms typically resolve within minutes to hours. TIA is a medical emergency because it signals high near-term stroke risk. The ABCD2 score estimates risk based on age blood pressure clinical features duration and diabetes. This patient has multiple risk factors. Up to 10-15% of TIA patients will have a stroke within 90 days with highest risk in the first 48 hours. She needs urgent evaluation including brain imaging carotid evaluation cardiac monitoring and initiation of secondary prevention.

question: A 34-year-old male is found on the sidewalk having generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity. Bystanders state the shaking has been going on for approximately 5 minutes. He has no medical alert bracelet and no one knows his history. He is unconscious with rhythmic jerking of all extremities jaw clenched eyes deviated upward and incontinent of urine. He is cyanotic around the lips. Vital signs obtainable are HR 128 SpO2 82% on room air. What are your immediate management priorities?
option_a: Wait for the seizure to stop then assess the patient
option_b: Protect from injury maintain airway suction if needed provide oxygen and prepare to administer benzodiazepine if seizure continues. Check blood glucose
option_c: Restrain the patient to prevent injury and insert an oral airway
option_d: Perform CPR because he is cyanotic
rationale: Active seizure management priorities are protecting from injury by moving objects away and padding if possible maintaining airway positioning to prevent aspiration suctioning secretions providing supplemental oxygen and checking blood glucose as hypoglycemia can cause seizures. Do not restrain forcefully or insert anything into the mouth. If seizure continues beyond 5 minutes or recurs without recovery it is status epilepticus requiring benzodiazepine administration. The cyanosis is from respiratory muscle involvement during the tonic-clonic activity not cardiac arrest so CPR is not indicated.

question: The patient continues to seize. Glucose is 118 mg/dL. You establish IV access with difficulty due to the movement. The seizure has now lasted 8 minutes total. According to the definition of status epilepticus what medication should be administered and what is the first-line dose?
option_a: Phenytoin 20 mg/kg IV is first-line for all seizures
option_b: Benzodiazepine is first-line. Options include midazolam 10 mg IM or 5 mg IV lorazepam 4 mg IV or diazepam 10 mg IV
option_c: Phenobarbital 20 mg/kg IV is first-line
option_d: No medication should be given until EEG confirms seizure activity
rationale: Status epilepticus is traditionally defined as seizure activity lasting more than 5 minutes or recurrent seizures without return to baseline. Benzodiazepines are first-line treatment. IM midazolam 10 mg is as effective as IV lorazepam 4 mg and is preferred when IV access is difficult. IV options include lorazepam 4 mg IV which has longer duration of action diazepam 5-10 mg IV which has rapid onset but shorter duration or midazolam 5 mg IV. Intranasal midazolam 10 mg is another option. If the first dose fails a second dose can be given. If benzodiazepines fail second-line agents include fosphenytoin levetiracetam or valproate.

question: After midazolam 10 mg IM the seizure stops. The patient is now postictal with GCS of 9 eyes closed responds to pain with withdrawal and makes incomprehensible sounds. He is breathing at 10 breaths per minute with SpO2 92% on room air. A friend arrives and states the patient has epilepsy and takes levetiracetam but ran out of medication a week ago. What is the expected postictal state and how should you manage this patient?
option_a: Postictal confusion is expected and may last 30-60 minutes. Support airway provide oxygen monitor closely and transport. He does not need another seizure medication now
option_b: He should wake up immediately after the seizure stops and prolonged confusion indicates stroke
option_c: Give another dose of midazolam to keep him sedated
option_d: The postictal state indicates brain death
rationale: The postictal state follows seizure termination and includes confusion lethargy combativeness and focal deficits like Todd paralysis that can mimic stroke. It typically lasts 5-30 minutes but can persist longer. This patient's GCS of 9 with slow respirations is consistent with postictal state plus benzodiazepine effect. Management includes airway support with positioning and possible adjuncts supplemental oxygen monitoring for recurrent seizures and supportive care during transport. Do not give more benzodiazepines unless seizures recur as respiratory depression is additive. Document the friend's information about medication noncompliance.

question: A 28-year-old female with known epilepsy has a witnessed seizure that lasts approximately 90 seconds then stops spontaneously. She is now postictal and slowly becoming more alert. Her husband states this is her typical seizure pattern and she has about 2 per month despite taking lamotrigine. Vital signs are BP 138/88 HR 96 RR 16 SpO2 97% on room air. Blood glucose is 102 mg/dL. She is becoming more oriented and asking what happened. She wants to refuse transport. Is this appropriate?
option_a: No. All seizure patients must be transported for CT scan
option_b: A brief self-limited seizure in a known epileptic with return to baseline who has a responsible adult present may refuse transport with appropriate documentation and instructions to follow up with her neurologist
option_c: Yes she can refuse but she should not take her seizure medication today
option_d: She must be placed on psychiatric hold for her safety
rationale: Not every seizure requires emergency department evaluation. A patient with known epilepsy who has a typical self-limited seizure returns to neurological baseline has no injuries has stable vital signs has a responsible adult present and can make an informed decision may refuse transport. Detailed documentation should include seizure description duration recovery baseline mental status normal exam vital signs medical decision-making capacity explanation of risks of refusal recommended follow-up with neurologist and signatures. She should take her seizure medication as prescribed.

question: A 6-year-old male had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes that has now stopped. His mother states he developed a fever of 103F today with a cold. He has never had a seizure before. He is now postictal but waking up and recognizes his mother. He has no history of epilepsy and takes no medications. Vital signs show temp 102.8F HR 118 RR 22 SpO2 98%. What type of seizure is most likely and what is the prognosis?
option_a: This is epilepsy and he will need lifelong seizure medication
option_b: This is most likely a simple febrile seizure which occurs in children 6 months to 5 years is associated with fever and has excellent prognosis. Most children do not develop epilepsy
option_c: This is meningitis until proven otherwise
option_d: Febrile seizures always indicate brain damage
rationale: Simple febrile seizures are common occurring in 2-5% of children between 6 months and 5 years of age. They are associated with fever typically from viral illness are generalized lasting less than 15 minutes and do not recur within 24 hours. Prognosis is excellent with no increased risk of developmental problems and only slightly increased risk of epilepsy about 2-4% versus 1% general population. Complex febrile seizures are focal prolonged greater than 15 minutes or recurrent and have higher risk of subsequent epilepsy. This child should be evaluated to identify the fever source but the seizure itself is typically benign.

question: You respond to a 45-year-old female who according to coworkers was staring blankly and not responding for about 2 minutes. She then began making lip-smacking movements and picking at her clothes. She did not fall or have convulsions. Now she appears confused and does not remember what happened. She has no seizure history. Vital signs are normal. Blood glucose is 94 mg/dL. What type of seizure did she most likely experience?
option_a: Grand mal seizure
option_b: Focal seizure with impaired awareness previously called complex partial seizure characterized by altered consciousness and automatisms without generalized convulsions
option_c: Absence seizure which only occurs in children
option_d: Pseudoseizure with psychiatric origin
rationale: Focal seizures with impaired awareness previously called complex partial seizures originate in one area of the brain and impair consciousness. They often involve automatisms which are repetitive purposeless movements like lip smacking chewing picking or fumbling. There is no generalized tonic-clonic activity. The patient typically has no memory of the event and may have a postictal period of confusion. These seizures commonly originate in the temporal lobe. New onset focal seizures in an adult require evaluation for underlying cause including brain tumor stroke infection or other structural lesion. She needs transport for evaluation including brain imaging.

question: A 55-year-old male with alcohol use disorder is found seizing by family. He reportedly stopped drinking 3 days ago after a week-long binge. The seizure stops after 2 minutes but he remains confused and tremulous. He is diaphoretic and tachycardic. Vital signs are BP 168/104 HR 122 RR 22 SpO2 96% Temp 100.4F. He is oriented only to person and appears to be seeing things that are not there. What is the cause of his seizure and what is your primary concern?
option_a: He has epilepsy exacerbated by alcohol
option_b: He is experiencing alcohol withdrawal seizures. Primary concern is progression to delirium tremens which has significant mortality without treatment
option_c: He had a seizure from alcohol intoxication
option_d: This is a febrile seizure from his low-grade fever
rationale: Alcohol withdrawal seizures typically occur 6-48 hours after last drink in patients with alcohol dependence. They are usually generalized tonic-clonic and may be single or multiple. The constellation of autonomic hyperactivity tremor hypertension tachycardia diaphoresis fever confusion and hallucinations suggests progression toward delirium tremens DTs which has 5-15% mortality without treatment. Benzodiazepines are the treatment of choice as they address the underlying GABA receptor dysfunction. This patient needs IV benzodiazepines aggressive hydration thiamine to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy and close monitoring in an ICU setting.

question: A 78-year-old female is brought from a nursing home for confusion. Staff states she was her normal self yesterday but today does not recognize them and is talking to people who are not there. She has a history of dementia hypertension and diabetes. Her baseline is oriented to person only and ambulatory with a walker. Today she is disoriented to person place and time and is picking at the air. Vital signs are BP 108/62 HR 102 RR 22 SpO2 94% on room air Temp 101.2F. What is the most likely cause of her acute change and how does delirium differ from dementia?
option_a: She has worsening dementia which is expected to fluctuate
option_b: She has delirium superimposed on dementia. Delirium is acute fluctuating and indicates an underlying medical cause such as infection. The fever and tachycardia suggest UTI or pneumonia
option_c: This is normal for someone with dementia
option_d: She is having a psychiatric emergency
rationale: Delirium is an acute confusional state characterized by fluctuating attention and awareness with rapid onset over hours to days. It indicates an underlying medical problem. Dementia is chronic progressive cognitive decline developing over months to years. Patients with dementia are at high risk for delirium from infections medications metabolic disturbances and other medical issues. This patient's acute change from baseline with fever tachycardia and borderline hypotension suggests infection likely UTI or pneumonia given her age and setting. She needs evaluation for the underlying cause not just treatment of confusion.

question: A 52-year-old male is found confused in his office. Coworkers state he was fine this morning but became increasingly confused and combative over the past 2 hours. He has no medical history that they know of. On exam he is diaphoretic with tremor of his hands. He is disoriented and agitated swatting at your hands when you try to examine him. Vital signs are BP 98/62 HR 118 RR 20 SpO2 98% on room air. Pupils are 4mm and reactive. Blood glucose is 38 mg/dL. What is the cause of his altered mental status and what is the treatment?
option_a: He is having a stroke and needs immediate CT scan
option_b: He has severe hypoglycemia causing neuroglycopenic symptoms. Treatment is IV dextrose D50 25g or 50mL followed by repeat glucose check
option_c: He is intoxicated and needs supportive care only
option_d: He is having a panic attack
rationale: Hypoglycemia causes altered mental status through two mechanisms. Autonomic symptoms from sympathetic activation include diaphoresis tremor tachycardia anxiety and hunger. Neuroglycopenic symptoms from brain glucose deprivation include confusion bizarre behavior combativeness seizures and coma. The brain depends on glucose and symptoms progress as levels fall. Treatment is glucose replacement with IV dextrose D50 25g for adults oral glucose if patient can swallow safely or glucagon 1mg IM if no IV access. Always check glucose in any patient with altered mental status as hypoglycemia is reversible but can cause permanent brain damage if prolonged.

question: A 67-year-old male presents with gradual onset of confusion over several days. His wife states he has been increasingly forgetful sleepy and unsteady on his feet. He fell yesterday. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and takes warfarin. He drinks 2-3 beers daily. On exam he is oriented to person only with slow speech. He has mild right arm weakness. Vital signs are normal. What diagnosis must be considered given his anticoagulation use and mechanism of mental status change?
option_a: Normal pressure hydrocephalus
option_b: Subdural hematoma. Elderly patients on anticoagulation are at high risk and may have minimal or no trauma history. Symptoms develop gradually over days to weeks
option_c: Wernicke encephalopathy from alcohol
option_d: Urinary tract infection causing confusion
rationale: Subdural hematoma occurs when bridging veins tear between the brain and dura. In elderly patients with brain atrophy these veins are stretched and vulnerable to injury from even minor trauma. Anticoagulation dramatically increases risk and patients may not recall any trauma. Symptoms develop gradually over days to weeks as the hematoma slowly expands including headache confusion personality changes and focal deficits. The triad of dementia gait disturbance and incontinence can mimic normal pressure hydrocephalus. CT scan will show crescent-shaped collection along the brain surface. Treatment may require surgical evacuation and anticoagulation reversal.

question: A 35-year-old female presents with severe unilateral throbbing headache behind her right eye that began 4 hours ago. She has nausea and has vomited once. Light and sound make the headache worse so she has been lying in a dark quiet room. She has had similar headaches before usually around her menstrual period. She took ibuprofen without relief. She has no fever or neck stiffness. Vital signs are BP 128/78 HR 76 RR 14 SpO2 99%. Neurological exam is normal. What type of headache is this and what treatments are appropriate?
option_a: Tension headache treated with acetaminophen
option_b: Migraine headache. Appropriate treatments include antiemetics like ondansetron or metoclopramide and analgesics like ketorolac. Triptans are also effective if available
option_c: Cluster headache treated with high-flow oxygen
option_d: Meningitis requiring immediate antibiotics
rationale: Migraine headaches are characterized by unilateral throbbing pain of moderate to severe intensity lasting 4-72 hours with associated nausea vomiting photophobia and phonophobia. They are often triggered by hormonal changes stress foods or sleep disturbance. Treatment in the acute setting includes antiemetics like ondansetron 4mg IV or metoclopramide 10mg IV and analgesics like ketorolac 15-30mg IV. Triptans like sumatriptan are migraine-specific medications that are highly effective if given early. Dark quiet environment and IV fluids for dehydration from vomiting are supportive measures.

question: A 42-year-old male presents with excruciating pain around his right eye that began 30 minutes ago. He is pacing and agitated unable to sit still. He states the pain is stabbing and rates it 10 out of 10. His right eye is red and tearing and his right nostril is congested and running. He has had similar episodes over the past two weeks always on the right side lasting about an hour each. Vital signs are normal. What type of headache is this and what is the most effective prehospital treatment?
option_a: Migraine headache treated with IV ketorolac and ondansetron
option_b: Cluster headache. High-flow oxygen 12-15 LPM via non-rebreather is highly effective and should be administered immediately
option_c: Tension headache treated with massage and relaxation
option_d: Glaucoma requiring immediate ophthalmology consult
rationale: Cluster headaches are characterized by severe unilateral orbital or temporal pain lasting 15-180 minutes occurring in clusters over weeks to months followed by remission periods. Unlike migraines patients are often agitated and pacing rather than lying still. Autonomic symptoms are ipsilateral to the pain and include lacrimation conjunctival injection nasal congestion rhinorrhea ptosis and miosis. High-flow oxygen 12-15 LPM via non-rebreather is first-line treatment and provides relief within 15 minutes in 70% of patients. The mechanism involves vasoconstriction and inhibition of trigeminal activation. Triptans are also effective.

question: A 58-year-old female presents with new onset headache over the past 3 weeks that is worse in the morning and when lying down. She has vomited several times particularly in the morning. She reports her vision has been blurry and she has noticed a change in her personality per her husband. She has no headache history. Vital signs are BP 148/88 HR 72 RR 16 SpO2 98%. On exam she has papilledema on fundoscopic examination and mild left arm weakness. What is the most likely cause of her headache?
option_a: New onset migraine
option_b: Intracranial mass lesion causing increased intracranial pressure. The progressive headache worse with recumbency morning vomiting papilledema focal deficit and personality change all suggest space-occupying lesion
option_c: Tension headache from stress
option_d: Medication overuse headache
rationale: New onset headache in middle-aged or older adults should raise concern for secondary causes. Red flags in this case include progressive worsening worse when supine due to increased ICP when lying flat morning vomiting from ICP increase during sleep papilledema indicating chronic elevated ICP focal neurological deficit and personality change. This constellation strongly suggests an intracranial mass lesion such as primary brain tumor or metastasis. She needs urgent CT or MRI imaging. If significant mass effect is present avoid actions that increase ICP and be prepared for possible herniation.

question: A 22-year-old male was involved in a motorcycle crash with significant head trauma. He was initially conscious at the scene but is now unresponsive. He is intubated for airway protection. On exam his left pupil is 7mm and fixed while the right is 3mm and reactive. He has extensor posturing to painful stimuli. Vital signs are BP 178/102 HR 58 RR being ventilated. What do these findings indicate and what is Cushing triad?
option_a: He has a cervical spine injury causing these findings
option_b: He has signs of uncal herniation with Cushing triad which is hypertension bradycardia and irregular respirations indicating severely elevated ICP with brainstem compression
option_c: These vital signs indicate hypovolemic shock
option_d: The unequal pupils indicate direct eye trauma only
rationale: Uncal herniation occurs when increased ICP forces the medial temporal lobe uncus through the tentorial notch compressing the midbrain and third cranial nerve. The ipsilateral pupil dilates due to CN3 compression and becomes fixed. Extensor posturing decerebrate indicates midbrain dysfunction. Cushing triad consists of hypertension widened pulse pressure bradycardia and irregular respirations. These are late signs indicating life-threatening ICP elevation and imminent brainstem death. Management includes elevating head of bed maintaining MAP for cerebral perfusion avoiding hypoxia and hypotension and brief hyperventilation to EtCO2 30-35 only if herniation signs are present.

question: You are managing the head-injured patient with signs of herniation. He is intubated and you are ventilating him. Your partner reports EtCO2 of 45 mmHg. What is the target EtCO2 for this patient and what is the rationale for brief hyperventilation in herniation?
option_a: Maintain EtCO2 at 45 mmHg which is normal
option_b: Target EtCO2 30-35 mmHg only if herniation signs are present. Hyperventilation causes cerebral vasoconstriction reducing ICP but also reduces cerebral blood flow so it is only used as temporizing measure for active herniation
option_c: Hyperventilate to EtCO2 of 20 mmHg to maximize ICP reduction
option_d: Hypoventilate to EtCO2 of 55 mmHg
rationale: Hyperventilation reduces PaCO2 which causes cerebral vasoconstriction decreasing cerebral blood volume and ICP. However excessive vasoconstriction reduces cerebral blood flow potentially worsening ischemia. Routine prophylactic hyperventilation worsens outcomes. Brief moderate hyperventilation to EtCO2 30-35 mmHg is reserved for patients with signs of active herniation as a temporizing measure until definitive treatment. Target EtCO2 for head-injured patients without herniation is 35-40 mmHg. Other ICP management includes head elevation to 30 degrees sedation avoiding hyperthermia and maintaining adequate MAP.

question: A 45-year-old female with a brain tumor presents with sudden severe headache vomiting and decreasing level of consciousness. Her family states she was fine yesterday. She is now responsive only to painful stimuli. Her left pupil is dilating. The neurosurgeon requests you give mannitol during transport. What is mannitol and what is its mechanism of action for reducing ICP?
option_a: Mannitol is an antibiotic that treats brain infections
option_b: Mannitol is an osmotic diuretic that draws water from brain tissue into the vasculature reducing cerebral edema and ICP. Dose is 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 minutes
option_c: Mannitol is a paralytic agent that reduces brain metabolic demand
option_d: Mannitol is a vasodilator that improves brain blood flow
rationale: Mannitol is an osmotic diuretic that does not cross the intact blood-brain barrier. It creates an osmotic gradient that draws water from brain tissue into the vasculature reducing cerebral edema and ICP. Effects begin within 15-30 minutes and peak at 1 hour. Dose is 0.5-1 g/kg IV typically as 20% solution given over 15-20 minutes. It is used as a temporizing measure in patients with signs of increased ICP and impending herniation. Side effects include hypotension from diuresis and electrolyte disturbances. It requires intact kidney function for excretion. Hypertonic saline 3% or 23.4% is an alternative osmotic agent.

question: A 25-year-old male dove into shallow water at a lake and struck his head on the bottom. Friends pulled him from the water and he is now on the beach. He is awake and states he cannot move or feel his arms or legs. He has neck pain. Vital signs are BP 82/50 HR 54 RR 14 SpO2 94% on room air. His skin is warm and dry. What type of shock is he experiencing and what is the underlying mechanism?
option_a: Hypovolemic shock from internal bleeding
option_b: Neurogenic shock from spinal cord injury causing loss of sympathetic tone. This results in vasodilation and bradycardia
option_c: Cardiogenic shock from cardiac contusion
option_d: Septic shock from aspiration
rationale: Neurogenic shock occurs when spinal cord injury above T6 disrupts sympathetic outflow causing loss of vascular tone and unopposed vagal activity. Signs include hypotension with bradycardia which distinguishes it from hypovolemic shock which causes tachycardia and warm dry skin from vasodilation rather than cold clammy skin of hypovolemia. This patient likely has a cervical spine injury causing quadriplegia and neurogenic shock. Treatment includes spinal immobilization IV fluids to treat hypotension and vasopressors like norepinephrine if fluids are insufficient. Atropine may be needed for symptomatic bradycardia.

question: The patient from the diving accident has been immobilized and you are transporting. He has weakness in both arms with hands weaker than shoulders and has some leg movement. Sensation is decreased in a cape-like distribution over shoulders and arms but intact in the legs. This pattern suggests incomplete spinal cord injury. What specific syndrome does this pattern suggest?
option_a: Anterior cord syndrome
option_b: Central cord syndrome with greater upper extremity weakness than lower extremity due to central gray matter injury. This typically occurs with hyperextension injuries
option_c: Brown-Sequard syndrome
option_d: Cauda equina syndrome
rationale: Central cord syndrome is the most common incomplete spinal cord injury. It occurs when damage primarily affects the central gray matter of the cervical spinal cord typically from hyperextension injuries. Because motor fibers to the arms are located more centrally in the corticospinal tracts than those to the legs arm weakness is greater than leg weakness. Sensory findings vary but may include a cape-like distribution. Central cord syndrome has the best prognosis of incomplete injuries with many patients regaining significant function particularly in the lower extremities. Bowel and bladder function often recovers as well.

question: A 32-year-old male was assaulted and stabbed in the back of the neck on the right side. He has weakness and loss of position sense in his right arm and leg but preserved pain and temperature sensation on the right. He has loss of pain and temperature sensation on the left side but strength and position sense are intact on the left. Vital signs are stable. What spinal cord syndrome does this represent?
option_a: Complete cord transection
option_b: Brown-Sequard syndrome or cord hemisection. Ipsilateral motor and proprioception loss with contralateral pain and temperature loss
option_c: Central cord syndrome
option_d: Anterior cord syndrome
rationale: Brown-Sequard syndrome results from hemisection or lateral injury to one side of the spinal cord often from penetrating trauma. The findings reflect the anatomy of spinal cord tracts. The corticospinal motor tract and dorsal columns proprioception and vibration have ipsilateral function meaning they do not cross until the brainstem so damage causes ipsilateral deficits. The spinothalamic tract pain and temperature crosses within 1-2 levels of entering the cord so damage causes contralateral deficits. Brown-Sequard syndrome has the best prognosis of all incomplete cord injuries with most patients regaining ambulatory function.

question: A 68-year-old male with ankylosing spondylitis is brought in after a fall from standing height. He fell backward and hit his head. He complains of severe neck pain and has weakness in all four extremities. He is having difficulty breathing with shallow respirations. Vital signs are BP 88/54 HR 52 RR 24 shallow SpO2 88% on room air. His spine is rigid and fused in a flexed position. Why is this patient at high risk for spinal cord injury and what is the respiratory concern?
option_a: His fused spine is protective and he is low risk
option_b: Ankylosing spondylitis causes brittle fused spine that fractures easily even from minor trauma. Cervical injury can affect phrenic nerve C3-5 causing diaphragmatic paralysis and respiratory failure
option_c: He has pneumonia from aspiration
option_d: His respiratory difficulty is from anxiety only
rationale: Ankylosing spondylitis causes spinal fusion with rigid brittle bones highly susceptible to fracture from even minor trauma. The fused spine acts like a long bone that can fracture at any point. These patients often have cervical involvement and even low-energy falls can cause unstable cervical fractures with cord injury. Respiratory difficulty from cord injury indicates high cervical involvement affecting the phrenic nerve C3-C5 which innervates the diaphragm. Loss of diaphragmatic function causes respiratory failure. Immobilization is challenging due to fixed kyphosis and may require padding to accommodate deformity.

question: You are caring for a patient with a known T4 spinal cord injury who lives in a group home. Staff called because he developed severe headache flushing above his nipple line profuse sweating and his blood pressure is 220/130. Heart rate is 48. He is anxious and complaining the headache is unbearable. Staff removed a kink in his urinary catheter just before symptoms began. What is this condition and what is the immediate management?
option_a: Hypertensive emergency from medication noncompliance
option_b: Autonomic dysreflexia which is a life-threatening hypertensive emergency in patients with injuries at T6 or above. Immediately sit him upright loosen clothing and identify and remove the noxious stimulus which was the kinked catheter
option_c: Stroke from the high blood pressure
option_d: Panic attack causing the symptoms
rationale: Autonomic dysreflexia AD occurs in patients with spinal cord injuries at T6 or above. A noxious stimulus below the injury level triggers massive sympathetic discharge that cannot be modulated due to the cord injury. This causes severe hypertension that can lead to stroke seizures or death. Common triggers include bladder distension from catheter obstruction fecal impaction pressure ulcers and tight clothing. Symptoms include pounding headache flushing and sweating above the injury level and piloerection. Management is to sit the patient upright immediately to lower BP and identify and remove the triggering stimulus. If BP remains severely elevated antihypertensives may be needed.

question: A 19-year-old male college student presents with fever severe headache and stiff neck that developed over the past 24 hours. He has vomited several times and the light hurts his eyes. His roommate was diagnosed with meningitis yesterday. On exam he has temp 103.2F and cannot flex his neck to his chest due to pain. He has a petechial rash on his trunk and extremities. Vital signs are BP 92/58 HR 128 RR 24 SpO2 96%. What type of meningitis is most likely and what is the significance of the rash?
option_a: Viral meningitis which requires supportive care only
option_b: Bacterial meningitis likely meningococcal given the petechial rash which indicates meningococcemia with sepsis and possible DIC. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate antibiotics and droplet precautions
option_c: Fungal meningitis
option_d: Migraine with photophobia
rationale: This patient has classic bacterial meningitis presenting with fever headache neck stiffness photophobia and vomiting. The petechial and purpuric rash is highly concerning for meningococcal meningitis Neisseria meningitidis. The rash indicates meningococcemia where bacteria are in the bloodstream and can progress rapidly to purpura fulminans DIC and Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome adrenal hemorrhage. This is a medical emergency with high mortality. Apply droplet precautions mask begin IV fluids for sepsis and transport emergently. In some systems prehospital antibiotics are administered.

question: You are transporting the patient with suspected meningococcal meningitis. Your partner is concerned about personal exposure. What precautions are appropriate and what prophylaxis might be needed for close contacts?
option_a: No precautions needed as meningitis is not contagious
option_b: Droplet precautions with surgical mask on patient and N95 or surgical mask on providers within 3 feet. Close contacts including EMS crew may need antibiotic prophylaxis with rifampin ciprofloxacin or ceftriaxone
option_c: Full airborne precautions with N95 and negative pressure
option_d: Contact precautions with gown and gloves only
rationale: Neisseria meningitidis is transmitted via respiratory droplets requiring droplet precautions. Place a surgical mask on the patient and providers should wear surgical masks or N95 when within 3 feet. Close contacts which includes household members roommates and healthcare workers with direct exposure to respiratory secretions should receive antibiotic prophylaxis. Options include rifampin 600mg BID for 2 days ciprofloxacin 500mg single dose or ceftriaxone 250mg IM single dose. Prophylaxis should be given within 24 hours of exposure and up to 10-14 days after exposure. Notify your supervisor and infection control about the exposure.

question: A 45-year-old male presents with confusion fever and bizarre behavior that has developed over the past 3 days. His wife states he has been acting strangely saying things that do not make sense having visual hallucinations and had a seizure this morning. He had flu-like symptoms a week ago. On exam he is disoriented with fever of 102F and has involuntary lip-smacking movements. He cannot follow commands consistently. Vital signs are otherwise stable. What condition is most likely and what area of the brain is typically affected?
option_a: Bacterial meningitis
option_b: Viral encephalitis most commonly herpes simplex encephalitis which has predilection for the temporal lobes causing psychiatric symptoms memory impairment and seizures
option_c: Drug intoxication
option_d: Schizophrenia with new onset psychosis
rationale: Encephalitis is inflammation of the brain parenchyma itself usually from viral infection. Herpes simplex virus HSV is the most common cause of sporadic encephalitis and has predilection for the temporal lobes. Symptoms include fever headache altered mental status personality and behavioral changes seizures and focal neurological deficits. Temporal lobe involvement causes psychiatric symptoms memory impairment and automatisms like lip smacking. This differs from meningitis which primarily causes meningeal irritation with neck stiffness. HSV encephalitis requires urgent treatment with IV acyclovir as delayed treatment increases mortality and neurological sequelae.

question: A 52-year-old female presents with progressive weakness over the past week. It started with tingling in her feet and has ascended to involve her legs and now her arms. She had a respiratory infection 2 weeks ago. She is having difficulty walking and raising her arms. Her voice sounds nasal. On exam she has symmetric weakness in all extremities worse distally with absent deep tendon reflexes. Sensation is intact. Vital signs show RR 22 with shallow breathing. She cannot count to 20 on one breath. What condition is this and what is the primary concern?
option_a: Stroke causing bilateral weakness
option_b: Guillain-Barré syndrome an ascending paralysis that can cause respiratory failure from diaphragmatic weakness. The breathing difficulty is an emergency requiring close monitoring and possible intubation
option_c: Multiple sclerosis flare
option_d: Myasthenia gravis crisis
rationale: Guillain-Barré syndrome GBS is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy causing ascending symmetric weakness with areflexia. It often follows viral infection by 1-4 weeks due to molecular mimicry triggering autoimmune attack on peripheral nerves. Sensory symptoms are typically mild compared to motor weakness. The critical concern is respiratory failure as weakness ascends to involve respiratory muscles. Inability to count to 20 or 10 on one breath negative inspiratory force less than 20 cmH2O or vital capacity less than 15 mL/kg indicates impending respiratory failure. Many patients require intubation. Treatment in hospital includes IVIG or plasmapheresis.

question: A 65-year-old female with a history of myasthenia gravis presents with increasing weakness and difficulty breathing over the past 2 days. She ran out of her pyridostigmine 4 days ago. She cannot hold her head up and has difficulty swallowing. Her voice is very weak and nasal. She is using accessory muscles to breathe. Vital signs are BP 138/88 HR 108 RR 28 SpO2 91% on room air. She appears exhausted. What is occurring and what is the concern about intubation medications?
option_a: She is having a myasthenia gravis exacerbation which will resolve with rest
option_b: She is in myasthenic crisis which is a life-threatening emergency requiring respiratory support. Avoid succinylcholine for intubation as myasthenia patients are resistant to depolarizing agents and sensitive to non-depolarizing agents
option_c: She has pneumonia requiring antibiotics
option_d: She is having a panic attack from being out of medication
rationale: Myasthenic crisis is acute severe weakness from myasthenia gravis causing respiratory failure. It can be triggered by infection medication changes stress or surgery. The bulbar symptoms dysphagia weak voice and respiratory muscle weakness are life-threatening. These patients often need intubation and mechanical ventilation. If RSI is needed avoid succinylcholine because myasthenia patients have fewer functional acetylcholine receptors and require much higher doses of depolarizing agents making response unpredictable. They are extremely sensitive to non-depolarizing agents requiring much lower doses. Use reduced rocuronium dosing if needed.

question: A 38-year-old female presents with facial weakness that she noticed when she woke up this morning. The right side of her face feels numb and she cannot close her right eye or raise her right eyebrow. Her smile is asymmetric. She denies weakness in her arms or legs and has no other symptoms. She has no medical history. Vital signs are normal. On exam she has complete paralysis of the right side of her face including the forehead. She cannot wrinkle her forehead on the right or close her right eye tightly. What is the most likely diagnosis and how does this differ from stroke?
option_a: This is an acute stroke and she needs emergent thrombolytic evaluation
option_b: This is most likely Bell palsy which is peripheral facial nerve CN7 palsy. The involvement of the forehead distinguishes it from stroke where forehead is spared due to bilateral cortical innervation
option_c: This is trigeminal neuralgia
option_d: This is facial trauma
rationale: Bell palsy is acute peripheral facial nerve paralysis usually idiopathic but associated with viral infection particularly HSV. The key distinguishing feature from stroke is forehead involvement. In Bell palsy the entire half of the face is paralyzed including forehead because all nerve fibers to that side are affected. In stroke or other upper motor neuron lesions the forehead is spared because it receives bilateral cortical innervation. Bell palsy patients also have no other neurological deficits. Treatment includes corticosteroids eye protection to prevent corneal injury and possibly antivirals. Most patients recover fully over weeks to months.

question: A 55-year-old female presents with sudden onset of severe vertigo that began when she sat up in bed this morning. The room is spinning and she has nausea. The vertigo worsens when she turns her head. She has no hearing loss tinnitus or neurological symptoms. She had a similar episode a year ago that resolved after a few weeks. Vital signs are normal. On exam she has horizontal nystagmus that increases when she looks to the right. There are no other neurological deficits. What is the most likely diagnosis?
option_a: Cerebellar stroke
option_b: Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo BPPV which is peripheral vertigo caused by otoconia in the semicircular canals. Episodes are brief triggered by position change and have no neurological deficits
option_c: Meniere disease
option_d: Vestibular neuritis
rationale: BPPV is the most common cause of vertigo caused by calcium carbonate crystals otoconia becoming displaced into the semicircular canals. Classic features include brief episodes less than 1 minute triggered by specific head positions such as rolling over or looking up no hearing loss and resolution with time or repositioning maneuvers. The Dix-Hallpike test can confirm diagnosis. The absence of other neurological deficits helps distinguish peripheral vertigo from central causes like stroke. Central vertigo often has associated symptoms like diplopia dysarthria dysphagia or limb weakness and the nystagmus may be vertical or direction-changing.

question: A 62-year-old male presents with acute vertigo that has been constant for 2 days. He also has nausea vomiting and difficulty walking. He has a history of hypertension diabetes and atrial fibrillation. On exam he has vertical nystagmus that changes direction when he looks in different directions. He has difficulty with tandem gait and finger-to-nose testing shows dysmetria on the left. Vital signs are BP 168/98 HR 88 irregular RR 16 SpO2 97%. What features of his presentation suggest central rather than peripheral vertigo?
option_a: The constant nature of symptoms suggests peripheral vertigo
option_b: The direction-changing vertical nystagmus cerebellar signs and vascular risk factors strongly suggest posterior circulation stroke rather than peripheral vestibular disease
option_c: The nausea confirms this is peripheral
option_d: His age confirms this is benign
rationale: Several features distinguish central from peripheral vertigo. Red flags for central causes include vertical or direction-changing nystagmus which peripheral lesions cannot produce cerebellar signs ataxia dysmetria inability to walk and vascular risk factors hypertension diabetes atrial fibrillation. The HINTS exam head impulse nystagmus test of skew can help differentiate with central vertigo showing normal head impulse direction-changing nystagmus and skew deviation. This patient has multiple features concerning for cerebellar or brainstem stroke and needs emergent evaluation with MRI as CT misses many posterior fossa strokes.

question: A 72-year-old male presents with tremor in his right hand that he first noticed several months ago. His wife says he has been walking more slowly and his posture is stooped. The tremor is present when his hand is resting in his lap but improves when he reaches for objects. His face appears less expressive than before. On exam he has rigidity in his right arm a shuffling gait and the tremor is rhythmic at about 4-5 Hz present at rest. What movement disorder does this suggest?
option_a: Essential tremor
option_b: Parkinson disease characterized by rest tremor bradykinesia rigidity and postural instability
option_c: Cerebellar disease
option_d: Huntington disease
rationale: Parkinson disease is a neurodegenerative disorder caused by loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra. Cardinal features are tremor bradykinesia rigidity and postural instability. The tremor is characteristically present at rest and improves with action which distinguishes it from essential tremor that worsens with action. Other features include shuffling gait masked facies reduced facial expression micrographia small handwriting and stooped posture. This patient demonstrates classic parkinsonian features. While not an emergency recognizing Parkinson disease helps with patient care decisions and communication with receiving facilities.

question: A 78-year-old male with Parkinson disease is brought in by his wife because he suddenly became very rigid and has a high fever. He appears confused and is barely responsive. His Parkinson medications were stopped 3 days ago when he was admitted to another hospital for surgery. Vital signs are BP 168/102 HR 118 RR 22 SpO2 95% Temp 104.2F. He has severe generalized rigidity and is diaphoretic. Lab values from the hospital show elevated CK. What condition has developed?
option_a: Parkinson disease progression
option_b: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome NMS or NMS-like syndrome from abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic medications. This is a medical emergency with high mortality
option_c: Heat stroke
option_d: Sepsis
rationale: Abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic medications in Parkinson patients can trigger a syndrome identical to neuroleptic malignant syndrome NMS. Classic NMS is caused by dopamine antagonists like antipsychotics but dopamine withdrawal has the same effect. Features include hyperthermia severe rigidity altered mental status autonomic instability and elevated CK from muscle breakdown. Mortality can exceed 10-20%. Treatment includes aggressive cooling IV fluids resuming dopaminergic therapy and possibly dantrolene for rigidity. This patient needs emergent ICU care. Prevention involves never abruptly stopping Parkinson medications even when patient is NPO.

question: A 76-year-old female with moderate Alzheimer disease is brought from her memory care facility for combative behavior. Staff states she became acutely agitated this afternoon hitting staff and yelling. This is not her baseline behavior. She has been incontinent today which is also unusual for her. Vital signs are BP 148/88 HR 98 RR 18 SpO2 97% Temp 99.8F. She is oriented only to name and cannot provide history. Urinalysis shows positive leukocytes and nitrites. What is the likely cause of her behavioral change?
option_a: Progression of her Alzheimer disease
option_b: Delirium superimposed on dementia likely from urinary tract infection. Acute behavioral change in dementia patients indicates an underlying medical cause
option_c: Psychiatric emergency requiring sedation
option_d: Medication non-compliance
rationale: Acute behavioral changes in dementia patients should not be attributed to dementia progression which occurs gradually over months to years. This acute change over hours indicates delirium from an underlying medical cause. UTI is the most common cause in elderly patients especially those with incontinence. Other causes include medications constipation fecal impaction dehydration infections pain and metabolic abnormalities. The mildly elevated temperature positive UA and new incontinence all point to UTI. Treatment addresses the underlying cause rather than just sedating the behavior. Chemical restraint should be used only if necessary for safety.

question: A 82-year-old male with Lewy body dementia is brought in for visual hallucinations. His wife states he has been seeing small children in the house that are not there and talking to his deceased brother. She is concerned because his hallucinations are worse than usual. He takes donepezil for dementia and has no other medications. On exam he is calm and pleasant. He describes the children he sees in detail. Vital signs are normal and he is afebrile. Neurological exam shows mild parkinsonism but is otherwise unchanged from baseline per wife. Is his presentation concerning and what medication class must be avoided in Lewy body dementia?
option_a: Visual hallucinations indicate psychiatric emergency requiring haloperidol
option_b: Visual hallucinations are a core feature of Lewy body dementia but worsening should prompt evaluation for underlying cause. Antipsychotics must be avoided as they cause severe sensitivity reactions
option_c: This is normal and requires no evaluation
option_d: He should be started on antipsychotics for the hallucinations
rationale: Lewy body dementia is characterized by fluctuating cognition visual hallucinations parkinsonism and REM sleep behavior disorder. Visual hallucinations are a core feature and patients often describe them in detail and may not be distressed by them. However worsening hallucinations should prompt evaluation for delirium from infection medications metabolic abnormalities or other causes. Critically antipsychotics are relatively contraindicated in Lewy body dementia because these patients have severe neuroleptic sensitivity and can develop irreversible parkinsonism neuroleptic malignant syndrome or death from standard antipsychotic doses.

question: A 58-year-old male with a history of ALS amyotrophic lateral sclerosis presents with increased difficulty breathing over the past week. He is wheelchair-bound with significant limb weakness. His speech is very slurred and he has difficulty managing his secretions. His wife operates a suction device frequently. He is on home BiPAP at night. Now he appears exhausted and is using accessory muscles to breathe even with BiPAP. Vital signs are BP 148/92 HR 108 RR 28 shallow SpO2 88% on room air improving to 92% with BiPAP. What is happening and what are the goals of care considerations?
option_a: He has pneumonia and needs aggressive treatment
option_b: He is experiencing progressive respiratory failure from ALS. Given his advanced disease goals of care discussion is critical as some ALS patients choose comfort measures over intubation and mechanical ventilation
option_c: He needs higher BiPAP settings only
option_d: His symptoms are from anxiety
rationale: ALS is a progressive neurodegenerative disease affecting motor neurons leading to weakness paralysis and ultimately death usually from respiratory failure. This patient shows signs of impending respiratory failure with accessory muscle use fatigue and hypoxia despite BiPAP. Goals of care are critical because once intubated many ALS patients cannot be weaned and face long-term mechanical ventilation or death. Many patients have advance directives specifying their wishes. Before intubation if possible clarify his wishes regarding mechanical ventilation. If he wants comfort measures focus on symptom relief. If he wants intervention proceed with intubation understanding the implications.

question: A 6-month-old infant is brought in after what the parents describe as a seizure. They report the baby's eyes rolled back and both arms and legs stiffened then shook rhythmically for about 2 minutes. The baby has been fussy with fever for 2 days from an ear infection. Temp is 102.4F. The baby is now alert playful and nursing normally. There is no family history of epilepsy. What is the most likely diagnosis and what features make this a simple versus complex febrile seizure?
option_a: The baby has epilepsy requiring anticonvulsant medication
option_b: This is a simple febrile seizure which is benign occurring in children 6 months to 5 years with fever. Simple features include generalized seizure duration less than 15 minutes and no recurrence within 24 hours
option_c: This is meningitis and the baby needs lumbar puncture
option_d: This is child abuse causing seizure
rationale: Febrile seizures are the most common seizure type in children occurring in 2-5% of children between 6 months and 5 years. Simple febrile seizures are generalized tonic-clonic last less than 15 minutes do not recur within 24 hours and occur in neurologically normal children. They have excellent prognosis with no increased risk of epilepsy and no need for anticonvulsant treatment. Complex febrile seizures have focal features duration greater than 15 minutes or recurrence within 24 hours and carry slightly higher risk of later epilepsy. This child's generalized 2-minute seizure with rapid return to baseline is simple febrile seizure.

question: A 4-year-old boy is brought in by his mother who states he has been having episodes where he stares blankly and does not respond for 10-20 seconds then returns to normal and continues what he was doing. These happen multiple times per day. He has no memory of the episodes. There is no convulsive activity and he does not fall. The episodes can be provoked by having him hyperventilate. He is otherwise developmentally normal. What type of seizure does this describe?
option_a: Focal seizures with impaired awareness
option_b: Absence seizures childhood absence epilepsy characterized by brief staring spells without convulsions or postictal period and provoked by hyperventilation
option_c: Psychogenic non-epileptic episodes
option_d: Normal daydreaming
rationale: Childhood absence epilepsy presents with brief typically 10-20 second episodes of behavioral arrest and unresponsiveness without convulsive movements or postictal period. The child stops what they are doing stares blankly then resumes activity with no awareness the episode occurred. Episodes can occur dozens of times per day and hyperventilation reliably provokes them which is diagnostically useful. EEG shows characteristic 3-Hz spike-and-wave pattern. Absence seizures respond well to ethosuximide or valproate and most children outgrow them by adolescence. These are easily missed or mistaken for inattention and may only be recognized when they affect school performance.

question: A 35-year-old male presents with 2 days of severe stabbing pain in his right cheek that occurs in brief episodes lasting seconds triggered by touching his face eating or talking. The pain is excruciating rated 10 out of 10 and he is afraid to eat or touch his face. Between episodes he has no pain. There is no facial weakness numbness hearing loss or other neurological symptoms. Vital signs are normal. What condition does this describe and what is the cause?
option_a: Dental abscess requiring antibiotics and extraction
option_b: Trigeminal neuralgia caused by vascular compression or demyelination of the trigeminal nerve CN5. The brief severe lancinating pain triggered by facial stimulation is pathognomonic
option_c: Cluster headache
option_d: Temporomandibular joint dysfunction
rationale: Trigeminal neuralgia also called tic douloureux causes severe lancinating pain in the distribution of one or more trigeminal nerve divisions typically V2 or V3. Episodes last seconds to 2 minutes are triggered by light touch to specific trigger zones and are separated by pain-free intervals. The pain is often described as electric shock-like or stabbing and is among the most severe pain syndromes known. It is usually caused by vascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root by the superior cerebellar artery or in younger patients by multiple sclerosis. Treatment includes carbamazepine oxcarbazepine or surgical decompression.

question: A 68-year-old male presents with fever and confusion. His wife reports he has had headache and fatigue for several days. Today he became confused and developed weakness on his right side. He recently returned from camping in the Midwest. On exam he has temp 102.8F flaccid weakness of the right arm altered mental status and fine tremor. There is no neck stiffness. Vital signs show tachycardia and mild hypotension. CSF would likely show lymphocytic pleocytosis. What infectious cause of encephalitis should be considered?
option_a: Bacterial meningitis requiring ceftriaxone
option_b: West Nile virus encephalitis which causes asymmetric flaccid paralysis encephalitis and can be acquired from mosquito bites during outdoor activities
option_c: Herpes simplex encephalitis
option_d: Rabies
rationale: West Nile virus is an arbovirus transmitted by mosquitoes endemic in much of the United States. Most infections are asymptomatic but about 1% develop neuroinvasive disease including meningitis encephalitis and acute flaccid paralysis. The flaccid asymmetric weakness resembles poliomyelitis and results from anterior horn cell damage. The combination of encephalitis with flaccid paralysis in someone with mosquito exposure history is characteristic. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis. There is no specific treatment only supportive care. Outcomes range from full recovery to permanent paralysis or death in severe cases.

question: A 45-year-old male presents with confusion and difficulty walking. He was found by police wandering in a parking lot appearing intoxicated. He has horizontal nystagmus in all directions of gaze ataxia and ophthalmoplegia with inability to move eyes laterally. He is confused and confabulating making up stories to fill memory gaps. He is malnourished appearing with signs of chronic alcoholism. Vital signs are stable except for tachycardia. What condition is this and what treatment is critical before giving IV fluids with glucose?
option_a: Alcohol intoxication requiring supportive care only
option_b: Wernicke encephalopathy caused by thiamine B1 deficiency. Thiamine must be given before or with glucose as glucose administration without thiamine can precipitate or worsen this condition
option_c: Drug overdose requiring naloxone
option_d: Hepatic encephalopathy requiring lactulose
rationale: Wernicke encephalopathy is caused by thiamine vitamin B1 deficiency usually in alcoholics or malnourished patients. The classic triad is encephalopathy confusion ophthalmoplegia lateral gaze palsy and ataxia though all three are present in only about one-third of cases. Thiamine is a cofactor for glucose metabolism and giving glucose without thiamine can exhaust remaining thiamine stores precipitating or worsening Wernicke encephalopathy. Always give thiamine 100-500mg IV before or simultaneously with glucose-containing fluids in alcoholics or malnourished patients. Without treatment Wernicke encephalopathy can progress to Korsakoff syndrome with permanent memory impairment.
